Dear Ms. Breeze:

r had made an inquiry regarding the legality and reporting
of expenses and gifts provided to public officials 
under the new
laws regulating gifts, honoraria and travef.

to 89507.) My understanding of your advice is summarized below.
Section 89502 prohibits state officials 
frorn receiving
honoraria. Subdivision (a) of section 89506, however, allows

speech, along wj-th lodging and rneals for the day before, the day
of and the day after the speech. (Section 89506 refers to these

According to our telephone conversation, it is the staff's

officiaL which do not fa11 explicitly within the exemptions
established by sectj-on 89506(a) are il1ega1 honoraria under
section 89502,
For instance, you advised that if a company holds a
conference and invj.tes a state official 
to make a speech at the
conference, it may pay for the officialrs 
transportation to and
from the conference, along with lodging and rneals at the
conference, as long as these palments are for rtravelr on the day
before, the day of, or the day after the official 
makes the
speech. Ho\"rever, a ticket for an entertainment activity held on
one of the free evenings of the conference, or an extra niqht,s

For reasons discussed below, we believe your advice
construes the term rthonorariumrr more broadly than was intended by
the Legislature.
The new Iaw, pre-existing provisions of the Act and the
regulations interpreting those provisions seem to establish a
hierarchy of pa)ments associated with a speech or appearance:
1. 
Honoraria are banned (S 89502);
2. 
Expenses ttdirectly in connectionrr with a speaking event
(e.g., absolutely necessary to the official's 
attendance) are
neither reportable nor subject to linits 
(2 cCR SS L8623,
tezzg);t

3. 
Payments, advances or reimbursements in addition to but
not including those expenses described in #2 above, which are rrin
connection withtt a speech, are reportable on the new- travel
schedule but are not subject to lirnits (5 89506(a));2 ana
4. 
Payments or expenses which are neither "directly in
connection withtr or rtin connection withtr a speech are gifts which
are reportable, subject to linits 
and which may subject the
official 
to disqual ification.
Your advice suggests that payments or exPenses in category #4
belong in category #1.'
We respectfully subnit that this characterization of
category f4 payments or expenses exceeds the charge of the new
statute. 
Section 89502 only prohibits state officials 
fron
accepting a ttpayment made in consideration for any speech gj-ven,
article published, or attendance at any public or private
conference, convention, meeting, social event, meat, or like
gathering"; i.e., state officials 
may not get paid for making
speeches or appearances. The statute does DqE, however, prohibit

these officials 
frorn accepting payments or expenses that are 4gE
made rrin consideration forrr a speech, article, or appearance,
i.e., gifts. 
It sinply linits the anount of these I'non-
honoraria/gifttr payments to 9250 per year.
The history of regulations l-8623 and l-8728 supports the
position that hosts may defray certain expenses of public
officials directly in connection with a speech. The commission
created exenptions for meals, acconnodations, incidentals and
intrastate transportation because it concluded that the public
official rs speech, appearance on a panel or participation in a
seminar constituted sufficient rrconsideration fortt these
expenses. Under the definition of gift in section 82028, these
benefits are therefore not gifts. 
If the official 
receives
payments rdhich exceed these regulatory parameters (for example,
accornmodations beyond what the Commission felt was rrnecessarytl
to the speaking appearance [2 ccR SS 18728]), he or she has
received a qift (albeit not subject to the new linit) 
-- but not
an honorariurn.
New section 87207 (c) (as amended by AB 4l-43, Ch. 1075, 1990
session) also reflects the conclusion that payrnents outside the
parameters of regulations 18623 and 1,8728 and sectj.on 89506(a)
are gifts but not honoraria. That section provides that
reimbursenents for travel which fall within the travel exernption
of section 89506 nay be reported by the officlal 
on a special
rrtravel scheduletr attached to his or her Statement of Economic
Interests;4 however, if he or she chooses not to use that
schedule, the filer ttshall disclose paynents for travel as a
gifttt (or Itas income, rr if the services provided vere equal to or
greater in value than the payments for travel) -- but in any case
not as an honor:ariun. (Emphasis added. )'
If palarents which falL outside the tixne frames in
regulations 7A623, l-t1728 and section 89506(a) are banned as
honoraria, as opposed to limited as gifts, countless unnecessary

uncertainties wiIl result. 
For instance: how many days before
or after a speech must an official anticipate, or wait, before he
or she may 1ega1ly receive even a nodest gift fronr a host, in
order to ensure that the gift will not be considered "in
consideration fortr the speech and therefore an illega1 payment?
What if the official 
speaks at one location, and the host
offers hirn or her free acconmodati,ons at another location two

l irnits?
what if the official 
attends an entertainment event held at

official 
accepts a ticket to an event after agreeing to make a
speech because everyone in the audience is going to the event?
For the reasons discussed, rre respectfully suggest that

cifts that are subject to the lirnits in section 89504
and 89505 i

3. 
Not honoraria subject to the ban in sections 89502 and
89503.
Such a rule is clearly supported by the new statute. 
(See
sections 89505(b) and 87207(c) .) If a state official 
wants to
renai.n after a conference for an extra day, and the host pays
that day's accommodations, the official 
has recei,ved a gift
subject to the $250 annual limit. 
Ruling that it is an
impermissible honorariurn clearly contradicts the Legislaturers
decision that the paynent be reported ras a giftr not as an
honorarium. The public purpose is no better served by allowing a
gift a week before an event but not on the day after the event.
The new limit of 9250 per calendar year prote-ts against any
possible undue influence.
I{e respectfully request reconsideration of this advice. 
We
would appreciate a written response on this issue even if you
choose not to reconsider it.


Blanca Breeze, Esg.
March 4, 1991

Thank you very much for your consideration.
very truly yours,

sections.question_synthetic: The letter challenges FPPC staff advice that payments to state officials for travel, meals, and entertainment outside explicit statutory exemptions constitute illegal honoraria, arguing instead that such payments should be classified as gifts subject to limits rather than banned outright.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming correspondence/challenge letter from a law firm (Nielsen, Merksamer, Hodgson, Parrinello & Mueller) requesting written confirmation and disputing FPPC staff advice. It is not an FPPC response letter. The document lacks traditional Question and Conclusion sections. The letter references oral advice from FPPC staff member Blanca Breeze and includes a summary table of the firm's proposed payment classification hierarchy. Document ID UNK-91-10393 suggests uncertain provenance.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: The letter challenges FPPC staff advice that payments to state officials for travel, meals, and entertainment outside explicit statutory exemptions constitute illegal honoraria, arguing instead that such payments should be classified as gifts subject to limits rather than banned outright.

embedding.summary: This is a law firm letter to FPPC staff disputing oral advice provided on February 13, 1991 regarding the proper classification of payments to state officials under new gift and honoraria laws (Gov. Code §§ 89501-89507). The firm argues that the FPPC staff's interpretation improperly classifies non-qualifying travel payments as banned honoraria when they should instead be treated as limited gifts.
